---
title: "Advanced Patterns"
description: "Plugin authoring, lazy loading, and other advanced techniques."
---

## Plugin authoring

Plugins are plain objects with a `name` and `setup` function. The setup function receives the app instance and can hook into any lifecycle event.

```js
const myPlugin = {
  name: 'my-plugin',
  setup(app) {
    app.on('ready', () => {
      // plugin logic here
    })
  }
}

const app = create({ plugins: [myPlugin] })
```

### Plugin options

Pass options to your plugin by wrapping it in a factory function:

```js
const myPlugin = (options = {}) => ({
  name: 'my-plugin',
  setup(app) {
    console.log('options:', options)
  }
})

const app = create({ plugins: [myPlugin({ verbose: true })] })
```

## Code splitting

MyProject supports lazy module loading out of the box. Use `app.lazy()` to defer expensive imports:

```js
const heavyModule = await app.lazy(() => import('./heavy'))
```

## Error handling

Catch errors globally with the `error` lifecycle event:

```js
app.on('error', (err) => {
  console.error('Caught:', err.message)
  // send to your error tracking service
})
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Unhandled errors inside async plugin setup functions will not automatically trigger the `error` event. Wrap async code in `try/catch` within your plugin.
:::

## TypeScript

MyProject ships with full TypeScript types. No `@types` package needed:

```ts
import { create, AppConfig } from 'myproject'

const config: AppConfig = {
  theme: 'cool',
  debug: true,
}

const app = create(config)
```
